selection.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?

And how?","opinion":"Well, I've been a hairdresser all my life in New York, so I'm not exactly an expert on this topic, but from what I see around me and hear at the cinema, there's definitely something to be said about the lack of women in science. It seems like most scientists I see on the screen are men, which is a bit odd because half the world is women, right? Maybe it's not so much that they're not interested, but perhaps they don't get the same support or encouragement as boys do when it comes to things like math and engineering.  My daughter has always been good at science, but I wonder if she'll face more challenges than her brothers did. It seems important to address this gap because, well, science is about understanding how everything works, including people, so not having half the population involved can't be a good thing. How? Well, maybe start early in schools, make sure both boys and girls are encouraged equally. And maybe have more role models for girls to see that it's possible. Maybe some kind of mentoring program where women who've made it in science can show younger girls the way. Also, perhaps companies and universities should take a look at their hiring practices to make sure they're not unconsciously favoring men. response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion: Well, I've been a hairdresser all my life in New York, so I'm not exactly an expert on this topic, but from what I see around me and hear at the cinema, there's definitely something to be said about the lack of women in science. It seems like most scientists I see on the screen are men, which is a bit odd because half the world is women, right? Maybe it's not so much that they're not interested, but perhaps they don't get the same support or encouragement as boys do when it comes to things like math and engineering.  My daughter has always been good at science, but I wonder if she'll face more challenges than her brothers did. It seems important to address this gap because, well, science is about understanding how everything works, including people, so not having half the population involved can't be a good thing. How? Well, maybe start early in schools, make sure both boys and girls are encouraged equally. And maybe have more role models for girls to see that it's possible. Maybe some kind of mentoring program where women who've made it in science can show younger girls the way. Also, perhaps companies and universities should take a look at their hiring practices to make sure they're not unconsciously favoring men. It might be a slow process, but it seems worth it for everyone's sake.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The perspective is shaped by the persona's role as a father wanting equal opportunities for his daughter, his lack of science background, and his love for cinema where he notices the underrepresentation of women in scientific roles. He believes that addressing the gap requires societal changes starting from education, role models, mentoring, and fair hiring practices.
response_parsed.tone: thoughtful, concerned, slightly pessimistic but hopeful for change
